definition: Tracking metrics is the practice of recording numeric evaluation results—loss, accuracy, RMSE, F1, and related scalars—across training steps or epochs, tagged by data split (train, validation, test) and consistent names. This topic is the canonical home for what to log, how to name metrics, and how to interpret scalar curves during ML experiments.
layman: Training a model is like practicing for a sport. You keep a training journal: how you did on practice drills (train), how you did on mock exams (validation), and—only once in a while—a real championship score (test). Tracking metrics is that journal: same scoreboard labels every day so you can compare runs fairly.
content:
## Why metric tracking exists

Supervised ML produces **numbers** at every training step: loss on the batch, accuracy on the epoch, F1 on validation. Without a disciplined log, teams cannot compare experiments, spot overfitting, or explain why one run beat another.

**Tracking metrics** answers three engineering questions:

| Question | What you log |
| --- | --- |
| Is the model **learning**? | Train loss / train metrics per step or epoch |
| Is it **generalizing** during development? | Validation metrics on held-out data |
| What is the **final** sign-off score? | Test metrics—logged rarely, with clear naming |

This topic is the **canonical home** for **logging train/val/test metrics**, **scalar curves over time**, and **metric naming conventions** in experiments. Related topics define **which splits exist** (Train/Val/Test Splits), **what F1 or RMSE mean** (Precision/Recall/F1, RMSE and MAE), **how loss relates to optimization** (Loss Functions), and **which tools persist logs** (MLflow & W&B).

### Train vs validation vs test metrics

Each split gets its own metric namespace:

```
train/loss          — optimization objective on training batches
train/accuracy      — training-set score (can look great while overfitting)
val/loss            — held-out loss for model selection & early stopping
val/f1              — primary selection metric during development
test/f1             — final report metric (log once per experiment, not every epoch)
```

**Train metrics** measure **fit** on data the model sees during `fit()`. They often improve monotonically even when generalization stalls.

**Validation metrics** measure **generalization during development**. Log them every epoch (or every N steps) to compare hyperparameters, trigger early stopping, and read learning curves.

**Test metrics** estimate **final generalization** after choices are locked. Logging test every epoch invites the same leakage as tuning on the test set—log test at checkpoint selection or run completion, not as a live dashboard.

### Scalar curves

A **scalar curve** plots one metric (y-axis) against **step** or **epoch** (x-axis). Typical patterns:

| Curve shape | Interpretation |
| --- | --- |
| Train ↓, val ↓ together | Healthy learning |
| Train ↓, val flat or ↑ | Overfitting — validation stopped improving while train keeps improving |
| Train flat | Underfitting, wrong LR, or broken pipeline |
| Noisy val curve | Small validation set or high-variance metric — smooth with moving average or log more frequently |

Log **both** train and validation on the **same chart** with distinct series names (`train/loss` vs `val/loss`) so divergence is visible at a glance.

### Metric naming conventions

Consistent names make experiment comparison possible across teammates and tools:

1. **Prefix by split**: `train/`, `val/`, `test/` (or `train_`, `val_` if your tool forbids slashes).
2. **Use canonical metric names**: `loss`, `accuracy`, `f1`, `precision`, `recall`, `rmse`, `mae` — match definitions in evaluation topics.
3. **One primary metric per task**: e.g. `val/f1` for imbalanced classification, `val/rmse` for regression — document it in the experiment config.
4. **Avoid ambiguous names**: `score`, `metric`, `result` tell you nothing six months later.
5. **Separate loss from business metrics**: `train/loss` (differentiable objective) vs `val/f1` (selection metric) — they need not move together.
6. **Version or phase tags when needed**: `val/f1@threshold_0.5` if threshold tuning is part of the experiment.

### Step vs epoch logging

| Granularity | When to use |
| --- | --- |
| **Per step** (batch) | Large models, long epochs, debugging instability — smooth val less often to reduce noise |
| **Per epoch** | Classical tabular/sklearn-style training — one validation pass per full pass over train data |
| **Per evaluation event** | Test metrics, benchmark suites, or expensive eval — log at end or on checkpoint |

Always log the **x-axis unit** explicitly (`global_step`, `epoch`) so curves from different runs align when comparing.

### What to log besides the headline number

Useful metadata paired with scalars:

- **Hyperparameters** (learning rate, batch size) — static per run, not per step
- **Split identity** — which validation fold or seed produced `val/f1`
- **Checkpoint id** when a metric is tied to a saved model
- **Class weights or threshold** if they affect reported metrics

### What this topic is not

- **Split design** — creating train/val/test partitions (Train/Val/Test Splits).
- **Metric definitions** — formulas for F1, RMSE, MAE (separate evaluation topics).
- **Tracker setup** — MLflow/W&B APIs, artifact storage (MLflow & W&B).
- **Loss function math** — why cross-entropy or MSE is minimized (Loss Functions).

The core idea: **record the right numbers on the right splits with stable names and time axes so experiments are comparable and curves reveal fit vs generalization.**
keyPoints: Log train metrics for learning signal, validation metrics every epoch for selection, and test metrics rarely for final reporting.
Scalar curves (train vs val over steps/epochs) reveal overfitting when train improves while validation stalls or worsens.
Use consistent split-prefixed names (train/, val/, test/) and canonical metric labels so runs compare cleanly.
The primary selection metric should match the task—val/f1 for imbalanced classification, val/rmse for regression.
Do not log test metrics every epoch as a tuning dashboard—that mirrors test-set leakage in metric form.
